docs.title: Akasha
docs.text: The shared cognitive fabric for intelligent agent systems.

docs.title: What is Akasha?
docs.text:
Akasha is a distributed, real-time key-value fabric designed specifically for multi-agent AI systems. It gives your agents:

  • Shared Memory — Working, Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural layers
  • Stigmergy — Pheromone-based coordination (no message passing needed)
  • Sub-millisecond latency — Faster than your LLM can think
  • CAS — Compare-And-Swap for conflict-free concurrent writes
  • Clustering — 3-node HA with CRDT + Raft consensus
  • Observability — Prometheus metrics, Grafana dashboards

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Get Akasha running in 30 seconds:

DockerPythonNode.jscurl 
docker run -d -p 7777:7777 -p 50051:50051 alejandrosl/akasha:latest

pip install akasha-client

from akasha import AkashaHttpClient

client = AkashaHttpClient(base_url="https://localhost:7777")
client.put("agents/planner/state", {"status": "thinking"})

state = client.get("agents/planner/state")
print(state.value)  # {"status": "thinking"}

npm install akasha-memory

import { AkashaHttpClient } from 'akasha-memory';

const client = new AkashaHttpClient({
  baseUrl: 'https://localhost:7777',
});

await client.put('agents/planner/state', { status: 'thinking' });
const state = await client.get('agents/planner/state');

# Write
curl -sk https://localhost:7777/api/v1/records/agents/planner/state \
  -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"value": {"status": "thinking"}}'

# Read
curl -sk https://localhost:7777/api/v1/records/agents/planner/state

docs.title: Why Akasha?
docs.text:
Most agent frameworks treat memory as an afterthought — a vector DB bolted on the side.

Akasha takes a fundamentally different approach: memory is the coordination layer itself. Agents don't need to talk to each other. They read and write to shared memory. They leave pheromone trails. They coordinate through the environment, not through messages.

This is stigmergy — the same mechanism ants use to build colonies without a central coordinator.

graph LR
    A[Agent Scout] -->|deposit pheromone| M[Akasha Memory]
    M -->|sense trail| B[Agent Analyst]
    B -->|write analysis| M
    M -->|read report| C[Agent Reporter]
    C -->|consolidate| M

    style M fill:#6c5ce7,stroke:#a29bfe,color:#fff
    style A fill:#00b894,stroke:#55efc4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#00cec9,stroke:#81ecec,color:#fff
    style C fill:#e84393,stroke:#fd79a8,color:#fff

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Component Purpose AkashaStore In-memory radix trie with versioning Pheromone Engine Stigmergy signals with exponential decay Nidra Memory consolidation (working → long-term) CRDT + Raft Distributed consensus + eventual consistency gRPC + HTTP Dual protocol access with WebSocket streaming

docs.title: Akasha — The Memory Layer for Intelligent Agents
docs.text: "What if your AI agents could remember, learn, and coordinate — without you wiring every connection?"

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
The enterprise AI landscape in 2026 looks like this:

Every organization is deploying multiple AI agents. A planner that schedules tasks. A researcher that retrieves documents. A coder that writes software. A monitor that watches production systems. Each agent is intelligent in isolation — but collectively, they are amnesiac.

docs.title: The Three Broken Promises of Multi-Agent Systems
1. Agents Forget Everything
docs.text: When Agent A discovers that "the client prefers summaries under 200 words," that insight dies with the session. The next time Agent B handles the same client, it starts from zero. Every interaction is a first interaction. There is no shared learning.

docs.title: 2. Agents Can't Coordinate Without a Director
docs.text: Today, if you want Agent A to pass context to Agent B, you build a pipeline. If Agent C needs to know what Agent A learned, you build another pipeline. For N agents, you need N² connections. This doesn't scale. And it makes your system brittle — one broken pipe, and the whole orchestra stops.

docs.title: 3. Infrastructure Wasn't Built for This
docs.text: Traditional databases store data. Vector databases store embeddings. Neither stores knowledge — the structured, evolving understanding that agents build over time. You end up duct-taping Redis, Pinecone, PostgreSQL, and a message queue together, and you still don't have a system where agents genuinely share cognition.

docs.title: The Cost of Inaction
docs.text:
Organizations running multi-agent systems today face:

Symptom Root Cause Business Impact Agents repeat the same mistakes No shared memory Wasted compute, user frustration Coordination requires custom code No stigmergic layer Months of engineering per workflow Agent context is lost between sessions Ephemeral state Degraded user experience over time Scaling from 3 to 30 agents breaks everything No distributed fabric Architecture ceiling

docs.title: The Solution: Akasha
docs.text:
Akasha is a distributed memory fabric purpose-built for multi-agent AI systems.

Think of it as the "shared brain" that sits behind all your agents — not replacing them, but giving them a place to store knowledge, recall experiences, and coordinate without explicit wiring.

docs.title: How It Works (30-Second Version)
docs.text:
┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐
│ Agent A │  │ Agent B │  │ Agent C │
│ Planner │  │ Coder   │  │ Monitor │
└────┬────┘  └────┬────┘  └────┬────┘
     │            │            │
     └────────────┼────────────┘
                  │
          ┌───────▼───────┐
          │    AKASHA     │
          │               │
          │  Working ─────│── What agents are doing right now
          │  Episodic ────│── What happened (event history)
          │  Semantic ────│── What agents have learned
          │  Procedural ──│── How to do things (workflows)
          │               │
          │  Pheromones ──│── Indirect coordination signals
          │               │
          └───────────────┘

Any agent can write what it learns. Any agent can read what others know. No pipelines. No message queues. No orchestrator deciding who talks to whom.

docs.title: The Bio-Inspired Difference
docs.text:
Akasha borrows from two powerful models in nature:

1. Human Memory — Your brain doesn't keep every sensory input forever. It consolidates: short-term impressions become long-term knowledge during sleep. Akasha does the same. A background process called Nidra (Sanskrit for "sleep") periodically compacts episodic events into semantic knowledge — automatically extracting patterns, discarding noise, and building lasting understanding.

2. Ant Colony Intelligence — Ants don't have a project manager. They coordinate through pheromones — chemical signals left in the environment. When one ant finds food, it leaves a trail. Others follow, reinforce, or let it fade. Akasha implements this as stigmergy: agents leave signals that others detect and respond to — without ever communicating directly.

The result: emergent coordination. Agents self-organize around tasks, priorities, and discoveries — just like a colony of 10,000 ants can build a bridge without a single blueprint.

docs.title: What Exists Today (v1.0.8)
docs.text: Akasha is not a roadmap. It is production software, running today.

docs.title: Core Capabilities
docs.text: Capability Status What It Means Shared Key-Value Memory ✅ Production Any agent reads/writes records via REST or gRPC Four Memory Layers ✅ Production Working, Episodic, Semantic, Procedural — each with appropriate retention Pheromone Trails ✅ Production Agents coordinate via environment signals, not direct messaging Memory Consolidation (Nidra) ✅ Production Automatic compression of episodic → semantic knowledge Optimistic Concurrency (CAS) ✅ Production Safe multi-agent writes without locks 3-Node HA Clustering ✅ Production CRDT-based replication, zero-downtime operations Auto-TLS ✅ Production Encrypted by default, zero configuration Dashboard ✅ Production Real-time visibility into memory state and cluster health Sub-Millisecond Latency ✅ Verified P50: 1.2ms. No LLM calls in the hot path

docs.title: Distribution
docs.text: Channel Link Docker Hub docker pull alejandrosl/akasha Python SDK pip install akasha-client Node.js SDK npm install akasha-memory GitHub github.com/ocuil/akasha-public Documentation ocuil.github.io/akasha-public Binary releases Linux x86_64 + macOS Intel

docs.title: Integration Ecosystem
docs.text:
Akasha integrates out-of-the-box with the major agent frameworks:

  • LangGraph / LangChain
  • CrewAI
  • AutoGen (Microsoft)
  • OpenAI Agents SDK
  • Google ADK
  • Semantic Kernel
  • Hugging Face smolagents

No vendor lock-in. Any framework. Any LLM. Any cloud.

docs.title: Performance Profile
docs.text: Metric Value Context Read latency (P50) 1.2 ms 40× faster than Mem0 Write latency (P50) 1.5 ms No LLM in the write path Binary size 25 MB Single binary, no JVM, no runtime Docker image 46 MB Smaller than a Node.js hello-world Memory footprint ~50 MB For 100K records Tests 163 passing Unit + integration, zero failures

docs.title: What Problem Does This Solve — Concretely?
Scenario: Customer Support AI
docs.text:
Without Akasha: - Agent A handles a ticket. Client mentions they prefer email over Slack. - Agent A closes the ticket. That preference is gone. - Next week, Agent B reopens a related ticket. Contacts the client on Slack. - Client is frustrated. "I already told you this."

With Akasha: - Agent A writes to memory/semantic/clients/acme/preferences: {"channel": "email"} - Agent B reads it before acting. Uses email. - Agent C (a summarizer) reads all client interactions from memory/episodic/clients/acme/** and consolidates patterns. - Nidra compacts 200 interaction logs into 3 semantic insights — automatically.

Zero custom pipelines. Zero coordination code. Zero data lost.

docs.title: Scenario: Multi-Agent Development Team
docs.text:
Without Akasha: - Coder agent generates code. Reviewer agent reviews it. But they don't share context — the reviewer re-reads everything from scratch. - A test agent finds a recurring pattern of null-check failures. That insight exists in a log file that nobody reads.

With Akasha: - Coder writes its working state to memory/working/coder/current-task - Reviewer reads it, already understands the intent - Test agent writes memory/semantic/patterns/null-check-failures with frequency data - Coder reads the pattern on the next task. Stops making that mistake. - A pheromone trail at pheromones/code-review-needed signals the reviewer without a message queue

The agents learn from each other without being told to.

docs.title: Where It's Going
docs.text: Akasha solves the foundational layer — the shared cognitive fabric. The roadmap extends into three horizons:

docs.title: Horizon 1: Foundation (Now → Q3 2026)
docs.text:
Make the fabric production-grade for early adopters

  • ✅ Core memory + clustering + CAS concurrency
  • ✅ Python SDK on PyPI
  • ✅ Node.js SDK on npm
  • ✅ Prometheus metrics + Grafana dashboards
  • ✅ Documentation site (MkDocs Material, 16 pages)
  • ◻️ Conflict resolution policies per namespace

docs.title: Horizon 2: Intelligence (Q3 → Q4 2026)
docs.text:
The fabric starts thinking

  • ◻️ LLM-powered consolidation (Nidra + language models)
  • ◻️ Automatic entity extraction and knowledge graphs
  • ◻️ Webhook/event streaming for external systems
  • ◻️ Multi-region clustering

docs.title: Horizon 3: Ecosystem (2027)
docs.text:
Akasha becomes the standard

  • ◻️ Managed cloud offering
  • ◻️ Marketplace for pre-built agent memory schemas
  • ◻️ Compliance and audit trails for regulated industries
  • ◻️ SDKs for Go, Java, Rust

docs.title: Competitive Position
docs.text:
Akasha Mem0 Letta Custom (Redis + Vector DB) Deployment Self-hosted, single binary Cloud-only Self-hosted DIY Latency 1.2ms (P50) ~50ms ~100ms Varies LLM dependency None Required Required None Clustering ✅ 3-node HA, CRDT ❌ ❌ DIY Agent coordination ✅ Stigmergy ❌ ❌ ❌ Memory consolidation ✅ Automatic ❌ Manual ❌ Cost at scale Fixed (infra only) Per-API-call Fixed High (engineering) Vendor lock-in None High Medium None

The key differentiator: Akasha is the only system that treats agent memory as a first-class distributed system — with concurrency control, clustering, bio-inspired consolidation, and stigmergic coordination. Everyone else is either a wrapper around vector search (Mem0) or a stateful agent framework (Letta).

docs.title: The Name
docs.text:
Akasha (आकाश) is a Sanskrit term from ancient Indian philosophy. It refers to the fundamental substance of the cosmos — the "space" or "ether" that everything exists within, and through which all information travels.

In Vedic cosmology, the Akashic Records are the universal memory — a field where every thought, action, and experience is inscribed. Not owned by any individual, but accessible to all.

That's what we're building: the shared memory field for intelligent agents.

Akasha is built with Rust for performance, designed with biology for intelligence, and distributed with intent for scale.

One binary. Zero dependencies. Infinite memory.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Akasha is built as a modular, layered system designed for sub-millisecond latency at the edge.

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph Clients
        P[Python SDK]
        N[Node.js SDK]
        C[curl / REST]
        G[gRPC Clients]
    end

    subgraph "Akasha Node"
        HTTP[HTTP Server<br/>Axum]
        GRPC[gRPC Server<br/>Tonic]
        AUTH[Auth Middleware<br/>JWT + API Keys]

        subgraph Core
            STORE[AkashaStore<br/>Radix Trie]
            PHER[Pheromone Engine<br/>Stigmergy]
            NIDRA[Nidra<br/>Memory Consolidation]
        end

        subgraph Cluster
            SWIM[SWIM Protocol<br/>Failure Detection]
            CRDT[CRDT State<br/>Eventual Consistency]
            RAFT[Raft Consensus<br/>Leader Election]
        end
    end

    P & N & C --> HTTP
    G --> GRPC
    HTTP & GRPC --> AUTH --> STORE
    STORE <--> PHER
    STORE --> NIDRA
    STORE <--> CRDT
    CRDT <--> SWIM
    SWIM <--> RAFT

    style STORE fill:#6c5ce7,stroke:#a29bfe,color:#fff
    style PHER fill:#e84393,stroke:#fd79a8,color:#fff
    style NIDRA fill:#00cec9,stroke:#81ecec,color:#fff

docs.title: AkashaStore
docs.text:
The heart of Akasha — an in-memory radix trie (prefix tree) that provides:

  • O(k) lookups where k is the key length (not the number of records)
  • Hierarchical paths — agents/planner/state naturally forms a tree
  • Glob pattern queries — agents/*/state, memory/**
  • Versioned records — monotonically increasing version numbers
  • Atomic CAS — Compare-And-Swap via If-Match headers

docs.title: Pheromone Engine
docs.text:
Agents coordinate without direct communication using stigmergy — the same principle ants use:

  1. Deposit a signal on a trail with an intensity and half-life
  2. Sense active trails to discover what other agents have found
  3. Signals decay exponentially — recent information is stronger
  4. Agents reinforce useful trails — emergent consensus

docs.title: Nidra (Memory Consolidation)
docs.text:
Inspired by how the human brain consolidates memories during sleep:

  • Working memory (TTL: seconds-minutes) → scratch pad for active tasks
  • Episodic memory (TTL: hours) → event logs, interactions
  • Semantic memory (permanent) → distilled knowledge, facts
  • Procedural memory (permanent) → how-to knowledge, workflows

Nidra periodically moves records from working → episodic → semantic based on access patterns and TTL policies.

docs.title: Clustering
docs.text:
Akasha supports 3+ node HA clusters using:

Protocol Purpose SWIM Failure detection, membership gossip CRDT Conflict-free replicated data types for eventual consistency Raft Leader election, configuration changes

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Agent
    participant H as HTTP/gRPC
    participant S as AkashaStore
    participant C as CRDT
    participant P as Peers

    A->>H: PUT /records/agents/planner/state
    H->>S: Insert (path, value, version)
    S->>S: Radix trie insert + version bump
    S-->>C: Replicate delta
    C-->>P: Gossip to peers
    H-->>A: 200 OK {version: 3}

    Note over A,P: Entire flow: <2ms

docs.title: Performance Characteristics
docs.text:
Operation Latency (P50) Latency (P99) Write 1.2ms 3.5ms Read 0.8ms 2.1ms Query (100 results) 2.5ms 8.0ms Pheromone deposit 1.0ms 2.8ms Pheromone sense 0.5ms 1.5ms

Benchmark conditions

Measured on a 3-node Docker cluster, single-threaded sequential operations over HTTPS with self-signed certificates.

docs.title: Compare-And-Swap (CAS)
docs.text: CAS provides optimistic concurrency control — multiple agents can read and write the same record without locking, and conflicts are detected at write time.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Agent reads a record and notes its version
  2. Agent computes its update
  3. Agent writes with If-Match: <version> header
  4. If the version still matches → write succeeds (version incremented)
  5. If another agent wrote first → 409 Conflict → retry with fresh version
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Agent A
    participant S as Akasha
    participant B as Agent B

    A->>S: GET /records/counter → version: 5
    B->>S: GET /records/counter → version: 5

    A->>S: POST (If-Match: 5) → count: 6
    S-->>A: 200 OK → version: 6 ✅

    B->>S: POST (If-Match: 5) → count: 10
    S-->>B: 409 Conflict ❌

    Note over B: Retry with version 6
    B->>S: GET /records/counter → version: 6
    B->>S: POST (If-Match: 6) → count: 11
    S-->>B: 200 OK → version: 7 ✅

docs.title: Usage
When to Use CAS
docs.text: Scenario Use CAS? Shared counters / state ✅ Yes Multiple agents updating the same record ✅ Yes Append-only logs ❌ No — use unique paths Agent's own private state ❌ No — only one writer Configuration changes ✅ Yes

docs.title: Memory Layers
docs.text: Akasha organizes agent knowledge into four cognitive layers, inspired by human memory systems.

docs.title: The Four Layers
docs.text:
graph LR
    W[Working Memory<br/>TTL: seconds] --> E[Episodic Memory<br/>TTL: hours]
    E --> S[Semantic Memory<br/>Permanent]
    E --> P[Procedural Memory<br/>Permanent]

    style W fill:#fdcb6e,stroke:#f9ca24,color:#000
    style E fill:#00cec9,stroke:#81ecec,color:#fff
    style S fill:#6c5ce7,stroke:#a29bfe,color:#fff
    style P fill:#e84393,stroke:#fd79a8,color:#fff

docs.title: Working Memory
docs.text:
Short-term scratch pad for active tasks. Auto-expires.

client.put("memory/working/task-42/context", {
    "current_step": "analyzing",
    "data_loaded": True,
}, ttl_seconds=60)

Path prefix: memory/working/ Default TTL: 60 seconds Use case: Active task state, intermediate results, scratchpads

docs.title: Episodic Memory
docs.text:
Event logs and interaction history. Medium retention.

client.put("memory/episodic/2024-01-15/user-login", {
    "user": "alice",
    "action": "login",
    "timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z",
}, ttl_seconds=3600)

Path prefix: memory/episodic/ Default TTL: 1 hour Use case: Conversation history, audit logs, event sequences

docs.title: Semantic Memory
docs.text:
Permanent knowledge. Facts, learned patterns, distilled insights.

client.put("memory/semantic/customers/acme-corp", {
    "preferences": {"channel": "email", "tone": "formal"},
    "industry": "manufacturing",
    "tier": "enterprise",
})

Path prefix: memory/semantic/ TTL: None (permanent) Use case: Knowledge base, learned preferences, domain facts

docs.title: Procedural Memory
docs.text:
How-to knowledge. Workflows, procedures, learned sequences.

client.put("memory/procedural/deploy-canary", {
    "steps": ["build", "test", "deploy-10%", "monitor-5min", "promote"],
    "rollback_on": "error_rate > 1%",
})

Path prefix: memory/procedural/ TTL: None (permanent) Use case: Standard operating procedures, learned workflows

docs.title: Querying Across Layers
docs.text:
# All working memory
working = client.query("memory/working/**")

# All memories about a topic
about_acme = client.query("memory/*/acme-corp/**")

# Layer stats
stats = client.memory_layers()
# → {"working": 38, "episodic": 467, "semantic": 50, "procedural": 2}

docs.title: Nidra: Memory Consolidation
docs.text:
The Nidra engine automatically consolidates memories:

  1. Working memory entries that are read frequently → promoted to Episodic
  2. Episodic entries with consistent patterns → promoted to Semantic
  3. Expired entries → garbage collected

This mirrors how the human brain consolidates short-term memories into long-term storage during sleep.

Etymology

Nidra (निद्रा) is Sanskrit for "sleep" — the state where memory consolidation happens.

docs.title: Pheromones & Stigmergy
docs.text: Akasha's pheromone system enables stigmergy — indirect coordination through the environment, without agents needing to communicate directly.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. An agent deposits a pheromone on a trail (path) with an intensity
  2. Other agents sense active trails to discover signals
  3. Pheromones decay exponentially based on their half-life
  4. Agents can reinforce trails by depositing on existing ones
graph TB
    S1[Scout Agent] -->|deposit<br/>intensity: 0.8| T1["Trail: tasks/urgent/bug-fix"]
    S2[Scout Agent] -->|deposit<br/>intensity: 0.3| T2["Trail: tasks/normal/refactor"]

    T1 -->|sense| W[Worker Agent]
    T2 -->|sense| W

    W -->|picks highest<br/>intensity trail| T1
    W -->|reinforce<br/>after success| T1

    style T1 fill:#e84393,stroke:#fd79a8,color:#fff
    style T2 fill:#636e72,stroke:#b2bec3,color:#fff

docs.title: Depositing Pheromones
docs.text:
Pythoncurl 
client.deposit_pheromone(
    trail="discoveries/weather-api",
    signal_type="discovery",
    emitter="scout-01",
    intensity=0.85,
    half_life_secs=1800,
    payload={"api_url": "https://api.weather.com"},
)

curl -sk https://localhost:7777/api/v1/pheromones \
  -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "trail": "discoveries/weather-api",
    "signal_type": "discovery",
    "emitter": "scout-01",
    "intensity": 0.85,
    "half_life_secs": 1800,
    "payload": {"api_url": "https://api.weather.com"}
  }'

docs.title: Sensing Pheromones
docs.text:
trails = client.sense_pheromones()
for t in trails:
    print(f"{t.trail}: intensity={t.current_intensity:.2f} ({t.signal_type})")

docs.title: Signal Types
docs.text: Type Use Case discovery "I found something useful here" success "This approach worked well" failure "Avoid this path" warning "Caution — proceed carefully" reinforcement "Confirming an existing signal"

docs.title: Decay & Half-Life
docs.text:
Pheromone intensity decays exponentially:

$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot 2^{-t / t_{1/2}}$$

  • A trail with intensity=1.0 and half_life_secs=3600 will be at 0.5 after 1 hour
  • This naturally prioritizes recent information over stale signals
  • Trails that are not reinforced eventually disappear

Choosing half-life

  • Short tasks (minutes): half_life_secs=300
  • Daily workflows: half_life_secs=3600
  • Persistent signals: half_life_secs=86400

docs.title: Clustering
docs.text: Deploy Akasha as a 3+ node HA cluster with automatic failover.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TB
    LB[Load Balancer<br/>HAProxy / Nginx] --> N1[Node 1<br/>Leader]
    LB --> N2[Node 2<br/>Follower]
    LB --> N3[Node 3<br/>Follower]

    N1 <-->|SWIM + CRDT| N2
    N2 <-->|SWIM + CRDT| N3
    N1 <-->|SWIM + CRDT| N3

    style N1 fill:#6c5ce7,stroke:#a29bfe,color:#fff
    style N2 fill:#00b894,stroke:#55efc4,color:#fff
    style N3 fill:#00b894,stroke:#55efc4,color:#fff

docs.title: Docker Compose (3-Node)
docs.text:
version: '3.8'
services:
  akasha-01:
    image: alejandrosl/akasha:latest
    environment:
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_ENABLED=true
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_NODE_ID=akasha-01
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_PEERS=akasha-02:7946,akasha-03:7946
    ports:
      - "7771:7777"

  akasha-02:
    image: alejandrosl/akasha:latest
    environment:
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_ENABLED=true
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_NODE_ID=akasha-02
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_PEERS=akasha-01:7946,akasha-03:7946
    ports:
      - "7772:7777"

  akasha-03:
    image: alejandrosl/akasha:latest
    environment:
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_ENABLED=true
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_NODE_ID=akasha-03
      - AKASHA_CLUSTER_PEERS=akasha-01:7946,akasha-02:7946
    ports:
      - "7773:7777"

docs.title: Verify Cluster
docs.text: curl -sk https://localhost:7771/api/v1/cluster/nodes | jq

docs.title: Single Node
docs.text:
docker run -d --name akasha \
  -p 7777:7777 \
  -p 50051:50051 \
  -v akasha-data:/data \
  alejandrosl/akasha:latest

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
version: '3.8'
services:
  akasha:
    image: alejandrosl/akasha:latest
    ports:
      - "7777:7777"
      - "50051:50051"
    volumes:
      - akasha-data:/data
    environment:
      - AKASHA_AUTH_ENABLED=true
      - AKASHA_JWT_SECRET=your-secret
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  akasha-data:

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
curl -s http://localhost:7777/api/v1/health | jq .status
# "ok"

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: Akasha exposes Prometheus metrics at /metrics for integration with Grafana, Datadog, or any Prometheus-compatible scraper.

docs.title: Prometheus Configuration
docs.text:
# prometheus.yml
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'akasha'
    scheme: https
    tls_config:
      insecure_skip_verify: true
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - 'akasha-01:7777'
          - 'akasha-02:7777'
          - 'akasha-03:7777'
    scrape_interval: 15s

docs.title: Available Metrics
docs.text: Metric Type Description akasha_records_total Gauge Total records in the store akasha_agents_total Gauge Registered agents akasha_pheromones_active Gauge Active pheromone trails akasha_memory_records{layer} Gauge Records per memory layer akasha_cluster_nodes{status} Gauge Cluster nodes by status akasha_crdt_tracked_paths Gauge CRDT tracked paths akasha_crdt_pending_deltas Gauge Pending sync deltas akasha_raft_commit_index Gauge Raft commit index akasha_raft_term Gauge Current Raft term akasha_raft_is_leader Gauge Whether this node is the leader akasha_nidra_is_leader Gauge Whether this node runs Nidra

docs.title: Grafana Dashboard
docs.text:
Import the ready-made Grafana dashboard:

  1. Download deploy/grafana-dashboard.json
  2. In Grafana → Dashboards → Import → Upload JSON
  3. Select your Prometheus data source
  4. Done! 12 panels showing all Akasha metrics.

The dashboard includes:

  • Stat panels: cluster health, records, agents, pheromones, CRDT sync, leader status
  • Time series: records over time, memory layers (stacked), cluster nodes, CRDT deltas, Raft consensus, pheromone activity

docs.title: Manual Check
docs.text:
curl -sk https://localhost:7777/metrics | head -20

# HELP akasha_records_total Total records in the store.
# TYPE akasha_records_total gauge
akasha_records_total 569
# HELP akasha_memory_records Records per memory layer.
# TYPE akasha_memory_records gauge
akasha_memory_records{layer="working"} 38
akasha_memory_records{layer="episodic"} 467
akasha_memory_records{layer="semantic"} 50
akasha_memory_records{layer="procedural"} 2

docs.title: LangGraph + Akasha Pipeline
docs.text: A complete example of 4 AI agents sharing knowledge through Akasha — zero orchestration.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    R[Researcher Agent] -->|write discoveries| M[Akasha Memory]
    M -->|read data| A[Analyst Agent]
    A -->|write analysis| M
    M -->|read report| W[Writer Agent]
    W -->|write draft| M
    M -->|read draft| C[Critic Agent]
    C -->|write feedback| M

    style M fill:#6c5ce7,stroke:#a29bfe,color:#fff

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Researcher gathers data → writes to memory/working/research/*
  2. Analyst reads research → writes analysis to memory/episodic/analysis/*
  3. Writer reads analysis → writes draft to memory/working/draft
  4. Critic reads draft → writes feedback to memory/semantic/feedback/*

No agent talks to another directly. They coordinate through shared memory.

docs.title: Run It
docs.text:
cd examples/langgraph-memory
pip install -r requirements.txt
python pipeline.py

docs.title: Source Code
docs.text: See the full example on GitHub: examples/langgraph-memory/

docs.title: Interactive Playground
docs.text: Try Akasha live in your browser — no install required.

docs.title: Open the Playground
docs.text:
The playground is a self-contained HTML page that connects to any Akasha instance:

→ Open Playground

Or serve it locally:

cd examples/playground
python3 -m http.server 8080
# Open http://localhost:8080

docs.title: Available Scenarios
docs.text: Scenario Description Hello Akasha Write + read + delete — the simplest interaction Memory Layers Write to Working, Episodic, Semantic, Procedural Optimistic Concurrency CAS race condition demo Pheromone Trails Deposit and sense coordination signals 3-Agent Pipeline Scout → Analyst → Reporter with stigmergy Latency Benchmark 50 writes + 50 reads with P50/P99 metrics

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Akasha uses a akasha.toml configuration file. All settings have sensible defaults — you can run Akasha without any configuration.

docs.title: Configuration File
docs.text:
Create an akasha.toml in the working directory:

[server]
grpc_addr = "0.0.0.0:50051"
http_addr = "0.0.0.0:7777"
name = "akasha"

[tls]
enabled = true
cert_path = "certs/server.crt"
key_path = "certs/server.key"

[auth]
enabled = true
jwt_secret = "your-secret-key-here"
default_user = "akasha"
default_password = "akasha"

[nidra]
enabled = true
interval_secs = 300
working_memory_ttl = 3600

[cluster]
enabled = true
node_id = "akasha-01"
bind_addr = "0.0.0.0:7946"
peers = ["akasha-02:7946", "akasha-03:7946"]

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Any config option can be overridden via environment variable:

Variable Default Description AKASHA_HTTP_ADDR 0.0.0.0:7777 HTTP listen address AKASHA_GRPC_ADDR 0.0.0.0:50051 gRPC listen address AKASHA_TLS_ENABLED true Enable TLS AKASHA_AUTH_ENABLED true Require authentication AKASHA_JWT_SECRET auto-generated JWT signing key AKASHA_CLUSTER_ENABLED false Enable clustering AKASHA_CLUSTER_NODE_ID hostname Node identifier AKASHA_CLUSTER_PEERS empty Comma-separated peer addresses

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Akasha supports two authentication methods:

docs.title: JWT Tokens
docs.text:
# Login to get a token
curl -s http://localhost:7777/api/v1/auth/login \
  -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"username": "akasha", "password": "akasha"}' | jq .token

# Use the token
curl -s http://localhost:7777/api/v1/records/test \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>"

docs.title: API Keys
docs.text:
Configure API keys in akasha.toml:

[auth]
api_keys = ["key-1-abcdef", "key-2-ghijkl"]

curl -s http://localhost:7777/api/v1/records/test \
  -H "X-API-Key: key-1-abcdef"

docs.title: TLS
docs.text:
Akasha generates self-signed certificates on first start. For production, provide your own:

[tls]
enabled = true
cert_path = "/path/to/server.crt"
key_path = "/path/to/server.key"

Self-signed certificates

When using self-signed certs, set verify_ssl=False (Python) or rejectUnauthorized: false (Node.js) in your SDK client.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Multiple ways to get Akasha running, from a single Docker command to a production HA cluster.

docs.title: Docker (Recommended)
docs.text:
docker run -d --name akasha \
  -p 7777:7777 \
  -p 50051:50051 \
  -v akasha-data:/data \
  alejandrosl/akasha:latest

Persistent storage

Mount a volume at /data to persist records across container restarts.

docs.title: One-Line Installer
docs.text:
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ocuil/akasha-public/main/deploy/get-akasha.sh | bash

This auto-detects your OS and architecture, downloads the correct binary, and installs to /usr/local/bin/.

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/ocuil/akasha-public.git
cd akasha-public
cargo build --release
./target/release/akasha

Requirements

Rust 1.75+ and a C linker are required for building from source.

docs.title: SDK Installation
docs.text:
PythonNode.js 
pip install akasha-client

Requires Python 3.9+. See Python SDK docs.

npm install akasha-memory

Requires Node.js 18+. See Node.js SDK docs.

docs.title: Enterprise Cluster
docs.text: For a production high-availability cluster, see the Clustering guide.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get Akasha running and execute your first operations in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: 1. Start Akasha
docs.text:
docker run -d --name akasha \
  -p 7777:7777 \
  -p 50051:50051 \
  alejandrosl/akasha:latest

Verify it's running:

curl -s http://localhost:7777/api/v1/health | jq

{
  "status": "ok",
  "name": "akasha",
  "version": "1.0.8",
  "records": 0
}

docs.title: 2. Write Your First Record
docs.text:
curl -s http://localhost:7777/api/v1/records/hello/world \
  -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"value": {"message": "Hello from Akasha!"}}'

docs.title: 3. Read It Back
docs.text:
curl -s http://localhost:7777/api/v1/records/hello/world | jq

{
  "path": "hello/world",
  "value": {"message": "Hello from Akasha!"},
  "version": 1,
  "content_type": "application/json"
}

docs.title: 4. Try With a SDK
5. Explore the Playground
docs.text: Open the Interactive Playground to try all Akasha features from your browser — no code required.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation options — Docker, binary, source, cluster
  • Core concepts — Memory layers, pheromones, CAS
  • API Reference — All 21 endpoints documented

docs.title: Node.js SDK
docs.text: TypeScript-first Node.js client for Akasha with HTTP and gRPC support.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: npm install akasha-memory

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
import { AkashaHttpClient } from 'akasha-memory';

const client = new AkashaHttpClient({
  baseUrl: 'https://localhost:7777',
  token: 'your-jwt-token',
  rejectUnauthorized: false,  // for self-signed certs
});

// Write
const record = await client.put('agents/planner/state', {
  status: 'analyzing',
  confidence: 0.85,
});

// Read
const state = await client.get('agents/planner/state');
console.log(state?.value); // { status: 'analyzing', ... }

// Query
const agents = await client.query('agents/*/state');

// Delete
await client.delete('agents/planner/state');

docs.title: CAS (Optimistic Concurrency)
docs.text:
import { CasConflictError } from 'akasha-memory';

const record = await client.get('shared/counter');

try {
  await client.putCas(
    'shared/counter',
    { count: record!.value.count + 1 },
    record!.version,
  );
} catch (e) {
  if (e instanceof CasConflictError) {
    console.log(`Conflict: v${e.expectedVersion} vs v${e.actualVersion}`);
    // Retry with fresh data
  }
}

docs.title: gRPC Client
docs.text:
For streaming subscriptions and higher throughput:

import { AkashaClient } from 'akasha-memory';

const grpc = new AkashaClient({ address: 'localhost:50051' });

await grpc.put('agents/worker/state', { status: 'idle' });

// Real-time streaming
for await (const event of grpc.subscribe('agents/**')) {
  console.log(`[${event.kind}] ${event.path}`);
}

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
// JWT Token
const client = new AkashaHttpClient({ token: 'eyJ0eXAi...' });

// API Key
const client = new AkashaHttpClient({ apiKey: 'your-key' });

docs.title: Python SDK
docs.text: Full-featured Python client for Akasha with sync, async, HTTP, and gRPC support.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install akasha-client

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
from akasha import AkashaHttpClient

client = AkashaHttpClient(
    base_url="https://localhost:7777",
    api_key="your-api-key",       # or use token="jwt-token"
    verify_ssl=False,              # for self-signed certs
)

# CRUD
record = client.put("agents/planner/state", {"status": "active"})
state = client.get("agents/planner/state")
client.delete("agents/planner/state")

# Query
results = client.query("agents/*/state")

# CAS
from akasha import CasConflictError
try:
    client.put_cas("shared/counter", {"n": 1}, expected_version=record.version)
except CasConflictError:
    pass  # retry

# Subscribe to changes
for event in client.subscribe("agents/**"):
    print(f"[{event.kind}] {event.path}")

docs.title: Async Client
docs.text:
from akasha import AsyncAkashaHttpClient

async def main():
    client = AsyncAkashaHttpClient(
        base_url="https://localhost:7777",
        token="your-jwt-token",
        verify_ssl=False,
    )

    await client.put("memory/working/task", {"step": 1})
    record = await client.get("memory/working/task")

Compatible with LangGraph, asyncio, and any async Python framework.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
# API Key
client = AkashaHttpClient(api_key="your-key")

# JWT Token
client = AkashaHttpClient(token="eyJ0eXAi...")

# Login to get a token
client = AkashaHttpClient(base_url="https://localhost:7777")
# POST /api/v1/auth/login

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: See the full REST API documentation for all endpoints.

docs.title: REST API Reference
docs.text: Akasha exposes a complete REST API over HTTP with 21 endpoints across 8 categories.

docs.title: Interactive Documentation
docs.text:
The full API is documented with OpenAPI 3.1 and can be explored interactively:

→ Open API Explorer (Redoc)

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text: https://localhost:7777/api/v1

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
All endpoints except /health require authentication:

# JWT
-H "Authorization: Bearer <token>"

# API Key
-H "X-API-Key: <key>"

docs.title: Health
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /health Full health check with cluster info GET /health/live Kubernetes liveness probe GET /health/ready Kubernetes readiness probe

docs.title: Records
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /records/{path} Read a record POST /records/{path} Write a record (+ CAS with If-Match) DELETE /records/{path} Delete a record

docs.title: Query
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /query?pattern=agents/* Pattern-based search

docs.title: Memory
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /memory/layers Memory layer statistics GET /nidra/status Nidra consolidation status

docs.title: Pheromones
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /pheromones Sense all active trails POST /pheromones Deposit a pheromone

docs.title: Cluster
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /cluster/status Cluster health GET /cluster/nodes Node list GET /cluster/sync CRDT sync status GET /cluster/raft Raft consensus state

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /metrics Application metrics GET /system/metrics System metrics (CPU, memory) GET /metrics (root) Prometheus scrape format

docs.title: OpenAPI Spec
docs.text:
Download the spec for import into Postman, Insomnia, or any OpenAPI tool:

→ Download openapi.yaml
